edges.explanation: 4.3 million homes missing with only 208,600 added in 2024/25 shows the scale of deficit that makes 300k/yr a minimum not an aspiration
4.3m deficit existing before the migration surge proves undersupply not immigration is the primary driver
The scale of the deficit is the primary evidence for the 80-year underbuilding claim
731 London starts and record-low permissions show the planning pipeline is nowhere near 300k delivery
Record-low permissions at 42,239 down 19% is direct evidence that the planning system constrains supply
Savills forecasting 42% shortfall against 1.5m target proves current policies cannot deliver at scale
Industry forecast falling 42% short directly supports the claim that no party is being honest about delivery
110,000 in temporary accommodation at £1.7bn/yr is a measurable consequence of decades of underbuilding
The human cost of 110k families in temporary accommodation demonstrates the need for supply to moderate costs and reduce homelessness
Government spending falling 45% during austerity proves the crisis was created by spending cuts not immigration
45% spending cuts are the primary evidence that government investment failure drives the crisis
Building rates dropping >1/3 after the 1947 Act is the 80-year dataset proving discretionary planning suppresses supply
The 80-year dataset directly justifies the rules-based zoning proposal as an alternative to the discretionary system
Building rate decline since 1947 is the structural evidence for the 80-year underbuilding claim
2m homes sold with only a fraction replaced and stock declining by 1.5m is the direct evidence that RTB devastated social housing
The 1.5m net loss of social homes is the case for mandatory 1:1 replacement and local suspension powers
Private sector peaking at 200k and 300k only with council building is unambiguous evidence the private sector cannot deliver alone
Historic record proving 300k requires public sector building means any party promising it without council building is dishonest
Historical precedent of council-led delivery is the evidence base for reviving council housebuilding at 50k+/yr
£1 saving £2-7 in health costs is the ROI evidence proving existing stock quality is a crisis worth investing in
The £2-7 return per £1 spent is the direct evidence that retrofit is the highest-ROI housing policy
Oldest stock in Europe with millions below EPC C proves the quality crisis is real and measurable
Scale of the EPC failure justifies the national retrofit programme targeting 1 million homes per year
Price-to-earnings doubling since 1990s confirms the affordability crisis that increased supply aims to moderate
Affordability collapse is a consequence of the 80-year underbuilding pattern
5 firms controlling 50% of output and releasing homes to maintain prices is structural evidence the private sector won't solve the crisis
Market concentration means private targets are constrained by the business model — developers don't build if it crashes their prices
The £39bn SAHP at 60% social rent provides the funding mechanism for the council housebuilding revival policy
300k being unachievable without public sector building supports the claim that parties promising it without council building are dishonest
Planning being a major barrier is the evidence base for zoning reform
Planning being necessary but not sufficient means parties claiming planning reform alone will deliver are wrong
Immigration being secondary and undersupply primary supports the 80-year underbuilding framing
RTB devastating social stock is the direct evidence for requiring 1:1 replacement and local suspension
Rent controls being useful only as interim stabilisers justifies the inflation-linked cap alongside supply increase
Private sector inability to deliver alone proves the dishonesty of targets without council building plans
Historic evidence that public sector building is essential directly justifies the council housebuilding policy
Supply moderating prices over decades means sustained building at scale is justified even if results take time
Quality crisis being measurable and costly supports retrofit being the highest-ROI policy
Measurable quality crisis directly justifies the national insulation programme
Council housebuilding revival is the specific policy response to the claim that private sector can't deliver alone
The 80-year underbuilding diagnosis means the policy response must be public sector building at historic scale
The planning system suppressing building since 1947 is part of the underbuilding claim that zoning reform addresses
Land value capture depends on the claim that the current system where developers capture planning uplift is part of the 80-year failure
The national retrofit programme depends on retrofit being the highest-ROI housing investment
Land value capture reform depends on the claim that 10-100x planning uplift currently benefits developers not the public
Rent stabilisation as interim measure depends on the assumption that supply will eventually moderate prices but takes decades
The inflation-linked cap design depends on the evidence that strict controls reduce supply while moderate controls don't
Abandoning mandatory targets and relying on brownfield and Help to Buy contradicts the evidence that public sector building at scale is essential
Maintaining Right to Buy without reform contradicts the 1:1 replacement policy given 2m homes sold without replacement
Labour's mandatory targets, £39bn SAHP at 60% social rent, and New Towns most closely match the evidence-based council building policy
NPPF reform moves toward but falls short of the rules-based zoning the evidence supports
380k/yr target with 150k social homes is the most ambitious commitment and closest to evidence-based scale
EPC C minimum for landlords directly aligns with the national retrofit programme
Local authority power to end RTB matches the evidence-based reform with local suspension
150k social homes/yr matches the scale of public sector building needed though funded differently
£29bn for EPC B exceeds the evidence-based minimum and Green retrofit commitment is the most ambitious
Green rent controls go further than the evidence supports — strict controls rather than the inflation-linked stabiliser the evidence favours
No targets, no social housing plan, and immigration scapegoating contradicts every element of the evidence-based building programme
4.3m home deficit from 80 years of underbuilding directly contradicts Reform's framing that foreign nationals cause housing pressure
Private sector never delivering 300k alone directly contradicts the Conservative approach of no mandatory targets and market-led delivery
Rules-based zoning would dramatically increase consented land, enabling the council housebuilding programme to find buildable sites
Land value capture would fund affordable housing from planning uplift, enabling public sector building without relying solely on borrowing
Modern Methods of Construction reducing build time by 30-50% enables faster delivery toward the 50k+/yr council building target
New build investment and retrofit investment compete for the same constrained public spending envelope
£39bn SAHP with 60% social rent is Labour showing it can be done at scale but still falls short of what 80 years of underbuilding requires
Price-to-earnings doubling creates the affordability pressure that makes rent controls politically attractive even where evidence is mixed
Market concentration with 5 firms capturing planning uplift is evidence that land value currently flows to developers not the public
RTB reform with 1:1 replacement depends on the finding that RTB devastated social stock without adequate replacement
MMC mandate depends on the finding that 300k/yr is unachievable with current methods — faster cheaper construction is needed

cross_domain_edges.explanation: The national retrofit programme is the same policy as the energy domain's National Insulation Service — housing quality and energy efficiency are delivered together
Cold damp housing driving respiratory illness contributes to NHS bed pressure and hospital admissions
110,000 in temporary accommodation at £1.7bn/yr is a direct welfare cost driven by housing failure
Housing undersupply being the primary driver not immigration reinforces the immigration domain's finding that underfunding not immigration causes service strain
The £5-10bn/yr council building programme requires fiscal space constrained by the overall tax and spending envelope
Insulating homes reduces fuel poverty which is a direct welfare and energy cost affecting the poorest households
